title: Tissue products and methods for manufacturing the same
abstract: Various implementations include a tissue product that includes a through-air dried tissue ply including a three-dimensional topography. The three-dimensional topography defines a first design element and a second design element encompassed within and discontinuous from the first design element. Various other implementations include a through-air dried fabric having a three-dimensional topography disposed thereon that forms the three-dimensional topography of the tissue ply. Various other implementations include a method of forming the tissue ply using the through-air dried fabric.
claims: Claims 1. A tissue product comprising at least one through-air dried tissue ply having a first surface and a second surface, the first surface having a three-dimensional pattern disposed thereon, the three-dimensional pattern comprising: a first design element, and a second design element encompassed within and discontinuous from the first design element. 2. The tissue product of claim 1, wherein the first design element defines a first enclosed area, and wherein the second design element defines a second enclosed area. 3. The tissue product of claim 2, wherein the second enclosed area is fully encompassed within the first enclosed area. 4. The tissue product of claim 1, wherein the first design element and the second design element are concentric. 5. The tissue product of claim 1, wherein the first design element and the second design element are circular. 6. The tissue product of claim 1, wherein the first design element and the second design element define segments that extend in a direction oblique to a machine-direction and oblique to a cross-machine direction. 7. The tissue product of claim 1, wherein the first design element defines a first depth of impression, and wherein the second design element defines a second depth of impression that is the same as the first depth of impression. 8. The tissue product of claim 1 , wherein the first design element and the second design element define a set of design elements, and wherein the three-dimensional pattern includes a plurality of sets of design elements. 9. The tissue product of claim 8, wherein adjacent sets of design elements are staggered in a machine direction and in a cross-direction. 10. The tissue product of claim 8, wherein the first design elements of adjacent sets of design elements intersect. 11. The tissue product of claim 8, wherein adjacent sets of design elements are discontinuous. 12. The tissue product of claim 8, wherein the plurality of sets of design elements are nonnesting. 13. The tissue product of claim 1, wherein a wet-out time of the tissue product is 5.1 seconds to 6.4 seconds. 14. The tissue product of claim 1, wherein an absorbent capacity of the tissue product is 9.2 g/g to 11.3 g/g. 15. The tissue product of claim 1, wherein a sheet caliper is 430 pm to 500 pm. 16. The tissue product of claim 1, wherein a stack caliper of ten sheets is above 4200 pm. 17. The tissue product of claim 1, wherein a firmness of the tissue product is 5.3 mm to 6.6 mm. 18. The tissue product of claim 1, wherein a sheet bulk of the tissue product is 9.5 cc/g to 11.7 cc/g. 19. The tissue product of claim 1, wherein a dry machine-direction tensile of the tissue product is 1287 g/3â to 1573 g/3â. 20. The tissue product of claim 1, wherein a dry cross-direction tensile of the tissue product is 688 g/3â to 841 g/3â. 21. The tissue product of claim 1, wherein a tensile ratio of the tissue product is 1.6 to 2.1. 22. The tissue product of claim 1, wherein a dry cross-direction TEA of the tissue product is 5.3 gf*cm/cm A 2 to 6.6 gf*cm/cm A 2. 23. The tissue product of claim 1, wherein a dry cross-direction tear of the tissue product is 7.4 cN to 9.1 cN. 24. The tissue product of claim 1, wherein a wet cross-direction tensile of the tissue product is 140 gf to 172 gf. 25. The tissue product of claim 1, wherein a geometric mean tensile of the tissue product is 940 g/3â to 1200 g/3â. 26. The tissue product of claim 1, wherein a GM slope of the tissue product is 10.4 kg to 12.8 kg- 27. The tissue product of claim 1, wherein a stiffness index of the tissue product is 9.9 to 12.2. 28. The tissue product of claim 1, wherein a wet burst of the tissue product is 157 gf to 191 gf. 29. The tissue product of claim 1 , wherein the first design element and the second design element arc molded by corresponding shapes defined in a through-air drying fabric. 30. The tissue product of claim 1, wherein the tissue product is free from embossments. 31. A through-air drying fabric for forming a tissue product, the through-air drying fabric comprising: a web contacting surface; and an additive manufactured, three-dimensional topography disposed on the web contacting surface, the three-dimensional topography defining a first shape, and a second shape encompassed within and discontinuous from the first shape. 32. The through-air drying fabric of claim 31, wherein the first shape and the second shape are concentric circles. 33. The through-air drying fabric of claim 31, wherein the first shape and the second shape define a set of shapes, wherein the three-dimensional topography includes a plurality of sets of shapes, and wherein the plurality of sets of shapes are non-nesting. 34. The through-air drying fabric of claim 31, wherein the first shape defines a first enclosed area, and wherein the second shape defines a second enclosed area. 35. The through-air drying fabric of claim 34, wherein the second enclosed area is fully encompassed within the first enclosed area. 36. The through-air drying fabric of claim 31, wherein the first shape and the second shape define segments that extend in a direction oblique to a machine-direction and oblique to a crossdirection. 37. The through-air drying fabric of claim 31, wherein the first shape defines a first pattern height, and wherein the second shape defines a second pattern height that is the same as the first pattern height. 38. The through-air drying fabric of claim 31, wherein the first shape and the second shape are included in a set of shapes, and wherein the three-dimensional topography includes a plurality of sets of shapes. 39. The through-air drying fabric of claim 38, wherein adjacent sets of shapes are staggered in a machine direction and in a cross-direction. 40. The through-air drying fabric of claim 38, wherein the first shapes of adjacent sets of shapes intersect. 41. The through-air drying fabric of claim 38, wherein adjacent sets of shapes are discontinuous. 42. The through-air drying fabric of claim 38, wherein the plurality of sets of shapes are nonnesting. 43. The through-air drying fabric of claim 31, wherein the through-air drying fabric is configured to produce the tissue product having a geometric mean tensile of the tissue product is 950 g/3â to 1200 g/3â. 44. The through-air drying fabric of claim 31, wherein the through-air drying fabric is configured to produce the tissue product having a sheet caliper is 430 pm to 500 pm. 45. The through-air drying fabric of claim 31, wherein the through-air drying fabric is configured to produce the tissue product having a stack caliper of ten sheets is above 4200 pm. 46. A method of forming a through-air dried tissue ply, the method comprising: providing a through-air drying fabric having a web contacting surface and comprising an additive manufactured, three-dimensional topography disposed on the web contacting surface, the three-dimensional topography defining a first shape and a second shape encompassed within and discontinuous from the first shape; forming an aqueous suspension of fibers; depositing the aqueous suspension of fibers onto a forming fabric to form a wet web; dewatering the wet web; transferring the dewatered wet web onto the web contacting surface of the through-air drying fabric; and through-air drying the dewatered wet web to form the through-air dried tissue ply, the through-air dried tissue ply including an imprinted three-dimensional topography imparted to the through-air dried tissue ply by the topography disposed on the web contacting surface of the through-air drying fabric, the three-dimensional topography defining a first design element corresponding to the first shape of the through-air drying fabric and a second design element corresponding to the second shape of the through-air drying fabric. 47. The method of claim 46, wherein the first design element defines a first enclosed area, wherein the second design element defines a second enclosed area; and wherein the second enclosed area is fully encompassed within the first enclosed area. 48. The method of claim 47, wherein the second enclosed area is fully encompassed within the first enclosed area. 49. The method of claim 46, wherein the first design element and the second design element are concentric. 50. The method of claim 46, wherein the first design element and the second design element are circular. 51 . The method of claim 46, wherein the first design element and the second design element define segments that extend in a direction oblique to a machine-direction and oblique to a crossdirection. 52. The method of claim 46, wherein the first design element defines a first depth of impression, and wherein the second design element defines a second depth of impression that is the same as the first depth of impression. 53. The method of claim 46, wherein the first design element and the second design element define a set of design elements, and wherein the three-dimensional topography includes a plurality of sets of design elements. 54. The method of claim 53, wherein adjacent sets of design elements are staggered in a machine direction and in a cross -direction. 55. The method of claim 53, wherein the first design elements of adjacent sets of design elements intersect. 56. The method of claim 53, wherein adjacent sets of design elements are discontinuous. 57. The method of claim 53, wherein the plurality of sets of design elements are non-nesting. 58. The method of claim 46, wherein a geometric mean tensile of the through-air dried tissue ply is 950 g/3â to 1200 g/3â. 59. The method of claim 46, wherein a sheet caliper is 430 pm to 500 pm. 60. The method of claim 46, wherein a stack caliper of ten sheets is above 4200 pm.
